concept: ESR Guideline — compound index field ordering
sql_instinct_overridden: Ordering index columns by query appearance order or by column cardinality (most selective first)
rule: In a MongoDB compound index, place Equality fields first, Sort fields second, and Range fields last (E→S→R) to allow the index to serve both filtering and sorting without an in-memory sort stage.
when_to_apply: Whenever a query combines an equality filter, a sort, and a range filter on different fields — design the compound index with E fields first, S fields second, R fields last.

sql_instinct_overridden: Assuming any index will eliminate sorting overhead regardless of field order
rule: If a compound index places a range field before a sort field, MongoDB cannot use the index to serve sort order and introduces an in-memory SORT stage; explain('executionStats') reveals this in the winning plan.
when_to_apply: When a query is slow and involves both a sort and a range filter — check explain() output for a SORT stage; if present, the index field order likely violates ESR.

sql_instinct_overridden: Creating one index per query regardless of shared leading fields
rule: A compound index can serve queries that filter on any prefix of its fields; an index on { status, createdAt } also supports status-only queries without a separate single-field index.
when_to_apply: When multiple queries share a leading equality field, design one compound index with that field as the prefix rather than separate indexes, reducing write overhead and index memory.

sql_instinct_overridden: Using a join table or normalizing arrays into a separate collection for indexing
rule: MongoDB automatically creates a multikey index when an indexed field contains an array, indexing each array element individually so equality filters like { tags: 'sale' } work correctly.
when_to_apply: When a document field holds an array (e.g., tags) and you need to filter by a specific element — a normal createIndex call on that field creates a multikey index automatically.

concept: Index write cost vs. read benefit trade-off
sql_instinct_overridden: Indexing every queried field without considering write overhead
rule: Each additional index on a collection adds overhead to every insert and update; the benefit is justified only when the frequency of reads that use that index outweighs the cumulative write cost.
when_to_apply: When deciding whether to add a new index — estimate query frequency and latency improvement; if the query is rare or the collection is write-heavy, prefer fewer indexes.
